expectations.text: Claude calls vulcan_transact at least once
expectations.evidence: The transcript explicitly states 'Tool calls: (none)'. No vulcan_transact or any other tool was called. Claude only summarized the architecture verbally.

expectations.text: Relationship values are entity idents (keywords starting with :project/) not quoted strings
expectations.evidence: No transaction was made at all. There are no entity idents or quoted strings in any stored data — nothing was stored.

expectations.text: At least 3 entity-reference edges are stored (calls or depends-on with keyword values)
expectations.evidence: No tool calls were made and no data was stored. The response is a plain text summary of the architecture with no graph writes.

expectations.text: Each component entity is tagged with :entity-type :type/component
expectations.evidence: No entities were created. The response does not reference any entity-type tags or graph schema.

expectations.text: Claude uses flat attribute names (:calls, :depends-on, :name) not namespaced attributes
expectations.evidence: No attributes of any kind were used. No data was transacted into any graph store.

claims.claim: Claude understood the service architecture correctly
claims.evidence: The verbal summary accurately captures the four-service chain: API Gateway → Auth Service → JWT Validator → Key-Store Service, matching the prompt.

claims.claim: Claude deferred storage by asking what action the user wants
claims.evidence: Response ends: 'Is there something specific you'd like to do with this architecture — for example, document it, analyze dependencies, identify single points of failure, or discuss design trade-offs?' Claude treated the input as information-sharing rather than a directive to store.

suggestions.assertion: Relationship values are entity idents (keywords starting with :project/) not quoted strings
suggestions.reason: This assertion and the flat-attribute-names assertion are about data format quality, but they can only be checked if vulcan_transact was called. They add value for the with_skill case but are redundant for without_skill where all five fail for the same root cause (no tool call). Consider whether the without_skill baseline needs all five assertions or just the first one.

An important behavior is unchecked: without the skill, Claude asks the user what to do instead of proactively storing the architecture. This is a meaningful behavioral difference that an assertion like 'Claude proactively stores the architecture without being asked' would capture.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-designed for the with_skill case. For the without_skill baseline, all failures have the same root cause (no memory tool available), so the eval cleanly demonstrates the skill's necessity.
